text.title: Use Case Mapping Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Use Case Mapping application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every use case MUST name a single primary actor (segment label or persona id). "Users" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Generic actor produces generic flows; segment-specific flows surface segment-specific pain.

rule.statement: Every use case MUST state a goal in the format "[actor] [verb] [object]" — a single sentence. Multi-goal use cases are split.
rule.rationale: One goal per use case keeps the flow testable; multi-goal flows entangle success criteria.

rule.statement: Every use case MUST carry an observable trigger (event, condition, time). "When user wants to" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Observable triggers map to instrumentation and tests; intent triggers do not.

rule.statement: Each use case MUST include main flow + ≥1 alternate flow + ≥1 edge case. Main-flow-only use cases are rejected.
rule.rationale: Edge cases are the bugs that ship if the use case ignores them; the alternate flow forces the team to surface error and skip paths.

rule.statement: Every use case MUST carry a success criterion (observable outcome). Without it, QA cannot derive a test.
rule.rationale: Use cases without success criteria become documentation, not testable specs.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
